name: Virginia
description: Virginia was a Roman plebeian woman whose tragic fate became a catalyst for significant political change in the Roman Republic. Her story, recounted by Livy, Dionysius of Halicarnassus, and others, centers around her beauty and virtue, which attracted the unwanted attention of Appius Claudius Crassus, one of the decemviri, a commission of ten men appointed to codify Roman law. Appius Claudius, consumed by lust, attempted to seize Virginia as his slave through a fabricated legal claim, exploiting his power and the legal ambiguities of the time. Her father, Lucius Virginius, a respected centurion serving in the Roman army, was summoned back to Rome to defend his daughter's freedom. Unable to secure a fair trial due to Appius Claudius's influence, and faced with the imminent threat of Virginia being enslaved and dishonored, Virginius made the agonizing decision to kill his own daughter to preserve her honor and freedom. This act of desperation sparked a popular uprising against the decemviri, leading to their downfall and the restoration of the tribunes of the plebs, who were crucial in protecting plebeian rights. Virginia became a symbol of Roman virtue, liberty, and the struggle against tyranny and abuse of power. Her death served as a powerful reminder of the importance of justice, the protection of individual rights, and the limitations of unchecked authority. She represents the resilience of the Roman people and their commitment to republican ideals.
subtitle: A symbol of Roman virtue and liberty
achievements: Sparked the downfall of the Decemvirate
Became a symbol of Roman virtue
Highlighted the importance of legal justice
Contributed to the restoration of plebeian rights

keyMyths.title: The Lust of Appius Claudius
keyMyths.description: Appius Claudius, a decemvir, became infatuated with Virginia and plotted to seize her as his slave through a false legal claim. This marked the beginning of Virginia's tragic story.

keyMyths.title: The Sacrifice of Virginia
keyMyths.description: To prevent Virginia from being dishonored and enslaved by Appius Claudius, her father, Lucius Virginius, killed her. This act was intended to preserve her honor and freedom.
keyMyths.source: Dionysius of Halicarnassus, *Roman Antiquities* Book XI

keyMyths.title: The Fall of the Decemvirate
keyMyths.description: Virginia's death ignited a popular revolt against the decemviri, whose abuse of power became unbearable. The uprising led to the restoration of the tribunes and a return to traditional Roman governance.

sources.author: Livy
sources.description: Provides a detailed narrative of Virginia's story and its political context.
sources.text: Provides a detailed narrative of Virginia's story and its political context.

sources.author: Dionysius of Halicarnassus
sources.description: Offers another account of Virginia's story, emphasizing the injustices of the decemvirate.
sources.text: Offers another account of Virginia's story, emphasizing the injustices of the decemvirate.

sources.author: Cicero
sources.description: Discusses the political implications of the Virginia incident and its impact on Roman republicanism.
sources.text: Discusses the political implications of the Virginia incident and its impact on Roman republicanism.
